coredata.dc:title: Embedding carbon quantum dots in cell envelops to accelerate electron transfer for microbial advanced oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ziyang
Yang, Chang
Song, Xingyuan
Yu, Qilin
Zhao, Zhiqiang
Zhao, Huimin
Zhang, Yaobin
coredata.dc:description: Microbial advanced oxidation, a crucial pathway for pollutant decomposition in the natural environment, is typically limited by microbial extracellular respiration. This is because the electro-inert cell walls of common microorganisms, such as Bacillus subtilis, hinder the cross-wall electron transfer necessary for reducing ferric species and inducing Fenton-like reactions. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CDs) were fed to promote the extracellular respiration of B. subtilis to strengthen the microbial advanced oxidation of aniline. The results showed that the CDs were embedded in the cell wall of bacterial cells to improve the extracellular electron transfer, which increased the •OH production (by 60.25%) and the aniline degradation. Meanwhile, the •OH generation mainly increased outside of the cell, which was beneficial for the degradation of pollutants and for relieving the damage of the radical to bacterial cells. The CDs induced the polarization of amide groups in the cell walls to maintain actively proton-coupled electron transfer to increase ATP generation by 39.28%. Feeding CDs provided a potential strategy to strengthen the extracellular respiration of common microorganisms and increase pollutant degradation via microbial advanced oxidation in the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Quantum Dots
Extracellular Respiration
Reactive Oxygen Species
Electron Transfer
Trans-cell-wall Electron Transfer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphologies of the CDs and CDs-fed B. subtilis. a: TEM images of CDs; b: Overall images of CDs-fed B. subtilis c: Topographies of CDs-fed B. subtills; d: Overall images of B. subtilis; e: Topographies of B. subtills.
Morphology of sections TEM. a: CDs-fed group; b: Control group with no CDs.

Production of and •OH confirmed by 5,5-dimethylpyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO)-trapped electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra. The fingerprint of four peaks are characteristic for •OH adducts, respectively. a: CDs-fed group; b: control group. c: Production of total •OH confirmed by dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO)-trapped and analysis by HPLC of B. subtilis with and without feeding CDs; d: 24-hour continuous hydroxyl radical production in CDs-fed and control group. e: Fe2+ concentration of CDs-fed Group and Control Group.
Intracellular •OH contents (H2DCFDA as the fluorescent probe that identifies •OH only after being hydrolyzed by intracellular hydrolases [36]) of bacterial cells of the Control and CDs-added group.
ATP generation and PCET analysis in the CDs-fed and control groups. a: ATP content detection of the bacteria; b: Kinetic isotope effect test of the bacteria.

Electrochemical characterization of CDs-fed group and control. a: EIS test of CDs-fed Group and control Group; b: CAFM topography of living B. subtilis on Indium-Tin Oxide glass with CDs fed; c: Electrical conductivity corresponding to the topography picture of living B. subtilis on Indium-Tin Oxide glass with CDs fed; d: CAFM topography of living B. subtilis on Indium-Tin Oxide glass without CDs fed; e: Electrical conductivity corresponding to the topography picture of living B. subtilis on Indium-Tin Oxide glass without CDs fed.

Electrochemical characterization of CDs-fed group and control. a: CV test of CDs-fed group and control under N2/O2 atmosphere; b: Electron-accepting capacity of the bacteria in the CDs-fed and control groups.
In-situ Fourier transform infrared spectra of the bacteria in the control and CDs-fed groups.

head.ce:title: Embedding carbon quantum dots in cell envelops to accelerate electron transfer for microbial advanced oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering Dalian University of Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering
Dalian University of Technology, Ministry of Education
School of Environmental Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering Dalian University of Technology, Ministry of Education, School of Environmental Science and Technology, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microbial advanced oxidation, a crucial pathway for pollutant decomposition in the natural environment, is typically limited by microbial extracellular respiration. This is because the electro-inert cell walls of common microorganisms, such as Bacillus subtilis, hinder the cross-wall electron transfer necessary for reducing ferric species and inducing Fenton-like reactions. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CDs) were fed to promote the extracellular respiration of B. subtilis to strengthen the microbial advanced oxidation of aniline. The results showed that the CDs were embedded in the cell wall of bacterial cells to improve the extracellular electron transfer, which increased the •OH production (by 60.25%) and the aniline degradation. Meanwhile, the •OH generation mainly increased outside of the cell, which was beneficial for the degradation of pollutants and for relieving the damage of the radical to bacterial cells. The CDs induced the polarization of amide groups in the cell walls to maintain actively proton-coupled electron transfer to increase ATP generation by 39.28%. Feeding CDs provided a potential strategy to strengthen the extracellular respiration of common microorganisms and increase pollutant degradation via microbial advanced oxidation in the environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial advanced oxidation, which is based on microbial extracellular respiration to produce •OH radicals, has been recently reported as an essential pathway for pollutant decomposition in the natural environment [1–3]. Under aerobic conditions, facultative microorganisms transfer electrons generated from intracellular metabolism to oxygen to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), like •O2 − and H2O2 [4–6]. Under anaerobic conditions, these microorganisms capable of extracellular respiration transfer intracellular electrons to extracellular Fe(III) species to generate Fe(II) [7–9], which further reacts with the generated H2O2 to initiate the Fenton reactions and produce the •OH radicals with strong oxidation capacity [10–12]. In wetlands, intertidal zones and soil, tides and photosynthesis induce periodic fluctuations in oxygen levels [13], making these locations critical sites for the microbial advanced oxidation of refractory organic pollutants [14–17].

However, facultative anaerobic microorganisms with strong extracellular respiratory capacities (like Shewanella), are not always predominant in the natural environment [18,19]. Instead, the majority of microorganisms either have no or only limited extracellular respiratory capacities due to their absence of unique electron transport units (such as MtrCAB of Shewanella) in their cell walls [20]. Under this circumstance, microorganisms only rely on the diffusion of electron shuttles, such as riboflavin [21], to transfer electrons across cell walls [22]. Meanwhile, the electro-inert peptidoglycan of cell walls, particularly in Gram-positive bacteria with a thick peptidoglycan layer [22], block the shuttle diffusion and electron transport, further hindering extracellular respiration [23]. This situation poses a challenge in triggering effective microbial advanced oxidation for the ·OH radical generation and pollutant removal in the environment.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), a low-cost conductive carbon nanomaterial, have been reported to strengthen the extracellular respiration of Shewanella species [24]. The nanoscale size of the CDs allows them to be embedded into cell walls, likely replacing electron transfer modules like MtrCAB to transport electrons outwards. Similarly, it is theoretically feasible to enhance extracellular electron transfer by embedding CDs into the cell walls of common bacteria lacking unique modules for trans-cell-wall electron transfer. Additionally, CDs were reported to interact with protein-like substances by chemical bonding [25,26], likely enhancing electron transfer. However, to date, there have been no studies on the impact of CDs on the microbial generation of ·OH radical and pollutant degradation.

In this study, Bacillus subtilis, a widely distributed Gram-positive bacterium that is capable of extracellular respiration only via secreting riboflavin as electron transfer shuttles [21,22], was selected as the model microorganism to investigate the effects of feeding CDs on its extracellular respiration and microbial advanced oxidation. Meanwhile, the ·OH radical generation with feeding CDs was investigated to elucidate the underlying mechanisms of CDs to impact the trans-cell-wall electron transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and purification of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, citric acid (0.4 g) was added into a 5 mL beaker (Φ2.0 cm × 5 cm) and heated in a heating shield at 200 °C until the citric acid was melted and gradually changed from a yellow color to a dark red liquid, which was then added into a 10 g/L NaOH solution and stirred until completely dissolved. The resultant supernatant was dialyzed by a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cut off 1 KD for 18 h, after cooling to room temperature. After that, the water-soluble CDs could be obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacillus subtilis strains and growth conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Bacillus subtilis BNCC 109047 strains, purchased from Bibio. Co., was initially caltured in an LB broth medium at 25 °C for activation [27]. Its growth was spectrophotometrically characterized using optical density at a wavelength of 600 nm (OD 600). When getting into the mid-exponential growth phase, the activated bacteria were transferred to culture bottles as the inoculum for domestication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Setup and operation of bio-chemical system
ce:section.ce:para: Six culture bottles each with 500 mL working volume (Φ8.6 cm × 18.2 cm) were used in this study. Three of these bottles were fed with the prepared CDs (10 mL), referred to as the CDs-fed group, and the other three bottles were not fed with the CDs, referred to as the CDs-free group. 1.2 g of hydromorphite was added to each reactor as the iron source for microbial extracellular respiration. After that, 400 mL of the culture medium was added to each bottle. For aerobic stage, air was aerated into the bottles with an aeration rate of 1 L/min for 60 min each day. After then, air aeration was replaced with CO2/N2 aeration (20 %:80 %, 1 L/min for 23 h each day) to form an anaerobic condition. The constitution of the culture medium is shown in Table S1.

Oxygen was intermittently aerated into the bottles to periodically serve as the terminal electron acceptor to form the aerobic condition. Under the anaerobic condition, the bottles were aerated with CO2/N2 (20 %:80 %). The aerobic duration and anaerobic duration were 1 h and 23 h per day, respectively.

The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer (Bruker, Elexsys II E500-CW-EPR) was used to qualitatively detect •OH radical production. To measure, 2 mL of the supernatant from the culture bottles were extracted and combined with 0.5 mL of 5,5-dimethylpyrrolidine-N-oxide (DMPO) solution (100 mM) within a reaction cell [28] (working volume of 10 mL). Prior to testing, all samples were subjected to redox oscillation (23 h N2 aeration and 1 h air).

The quantitative analysis of the •OH radicals production was conducted with High-performance liquid chromatography (Shimadzu, LC-2050C). 5 mL of supernatant (centrifuged at 2100 g/rcf) from two groups were combined with 5 mL of DMSO (100 mM) in order to trap the produced •OH radicals [29]. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was utilised as a trapping agent for the reaction with •OH radicals for the quantitative production of formaldehyde (HCHO), which was subsequently derivatised with 2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH) to yield HCHO-DNPH [30] (a single and stable product). All technical abbreviations have been defined upon first usage. After a full reaction period of ten minutes,1 mL of the sample was collected and combined it with 5 mL of the H3PO4-NaH2PO4 buffer solution along with 2.7 mL of DNPH solution (1 mM) [29]. The mixture was heated in a water bath at 50℃ for 30 min, subsequently cooled to room temperature, and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane for HPLC analysis.

The enzyme-labeled instrument was used to perform a semi-quantitative analysis of intracellular •OH production. The bacteria were inoculated into 96-well plates with 50 µL of bacterial solution per well and subsequently incubated for 30 min. Afterwards, the wells were washed twice with Earle's equilibrium salt solution, and each well was subsequently treated with 50 µL of 25 μM DCFH-DA followed by a 30-minute incubation period [31]. After incubation, the sample was rinsed twice using Earles equilibrium salt solution, and fluorescence was observed using an enzyme-labeled device with excitation wavelength at 488 nm and emission wavelength at 525 nm [32].

Typically, 1 mL of purified aqueous CDs solution as mentioned above was taken out and diluted with 9 mL of high-purity water. 2.5 μL of the obtained diluent was taken out and added dropwise to an ultrathin carbon film (200 meshs, Φ3.05 mm, Electron Microscopy CHNA) for natural air drying, which was then characterized with transmission electron microscopy (JEM-F200, Jeol, Japan) for morphological analysis.

The synthesized CDs sample was characterized with transmission electron microscopy (TEM). From Fig. 1 a, quasi-spherical dots with an average diameter of approximately 2.5 nm were clearly observed. The TEM micrograph also showed a clear crystalline structure. After feeding the synthesized CDs to B. subtilis for 12 h, black dots were detected on the surface of the bacterial cells (Fig. 1b, c). Comparatively, no dots were found on the bacterial cells of the control group without feeding CDs, and the smooth cell wall was visible on the focal magnification (Fig. 1d, e).

Bacterial sections were also characterized by TEM to demonstrate whether the CDs were embedded in the cell wall. Black dots were found to be embedded within the cell of B. subtilis (Fig. 2 a), while no CDs-like materials were found in the control without feeding CDs (Fig. 2b). These results indicated that the CDs were attached on and embedded in the cell wall with feeding CDs, which might make the embedded CDs to serve as electron mediators and get engaged in the extracellular respiration.

B. subtilis were cultured under the periodically aerobic/anaerobic conditions to observe the possible •OH generation during the redox oscillations. Four distinct fingerprint peaks corresponding to •OH were detected in the EPR spectra of the bacterial solution of CDs-fed B. subtilis (Fig. 3 a). Comparatively, no significant •OH signal was found in the bacterial solution of the control group without feeding CDs (Fig. 3b). The addition of CDs enhanced the generation of respiration-based •OH in the culture of B. subtilis under the redox oscillations.

Generally, the thicker peptidoglycan in the cell wall of B. subtilis hinders the diffusion of electron shuttle to transport electron outwards [33–35]. The conductive CDs embedded in the cell wall of B. subtilis, might perform the similar function of the electron transfer components as the MtrCAB of Shewanella [20], which directly transported electrons and then accelerated cross-wall electron transfer to promote the •OH radical production and aniline degradation. Consistently, the •OH concentration in the CDs-fed group (0.258 mg/L) was 60.25 % higher than the CDs-free control group (0.161 mg/L, Fig. 3c). It was found that the hydroxyl radicals in the CDs-fed group was 0.189 mg/L in the end of the air aeration (i.e., aerobic condition), higher than that of the control group (0.174 mg/L). After then, the concentration of hydroxyl radicals of the two groups all decreased under the anaerobic condition, but the hydroxyl radical concentration of the CDs-fed group was still higher than that of the control group (Fig. 3.d). After the anaerobic cultivation of 23 h, the Fe2+ concentration of the CDs-added group (0.21085) was significantly higher than that of the control (0.05378 mg/L), indicating that the dissimilatory iron reduction of the CDs-fed group was promoted to generate the Fe2+ as the catalyst for Fenton-like reaction (Fig. 3e). And the enhanced microbial extracellular respiration (dissimilatory iron reduction) was accounted for the increased •OH concentration.

Furthermore, the bacterial solutions of the two groups were cultured to investigate the decomposition of aniline under periodically switching anaerobic and aerobic conditions (1 h of O2 aeration and 23 h of N2 aeration per day). From Fig. S1, the decomposition of aniline in the CDs-fed group was faster. Particularly, on day 16, the concentration of aniline in the CDs-fed group was only 8.17 mg/L, compared with 26.01 mg/L in the control group. It demonstrated that the addition of CDs accelerated the degradation of aniline, which could be ascribed that the added CDs promoted the •OH radical generation (Fig. 3c) and the microbial advanced oxidation.

The embedded CDs in the cell benefitted to electron transfer outwards, which might cause more oxygen to be reduced extracellularly. To verify it, the intracellular •OH production of B. subtilis was semiquantitatively analyzed by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the complex formed by DCF [36] entering the cell and binding to •OH. Results showed that the intracellular •OH production of the CDs-fed group and control group were almost at the same level, with the average fluorescence intensity of 1192.89 a.u. and 1200.87 a.u., respectively(Fig. 4 ).

Together with the significantly higher production of total •OH in the CDs-fed group (Fig. 3c), the intracellular •OH production of the two groups at almost the same level meant that the extracellular •OH production increased with the addition of CDs, which was related that the CDs embedded in the cell promoted the electron transfer outside. The •OH production increased mainly in the extracellular, beneficial for the degradation of pollutants and for mitigating the damage to bacterial cells. Consistently, the cell viabilities of B. subtilis in the CDs-fed group and control group was 89.4 % and 81.0 %, respectively (Fig. S2).

Oxygen reduction can occur either intracellularly or extracellularly, with the electrons involved needing to pass through Complex I to III of the cell’s respiration chains [37]. In extracellular respiration, electrons from Complex I, II and III can be transferred directly to the outside detouring Complex IV [38], which may lead to the decrease of the proton out-flow and the proton motive force for ATP generation. With the CDs addition, oxygen reduction more likely occurred in the outside of cells, thereby possibly decreasing the energy generation from aerobic respiration.

Reversely, the enzyme activity test showed that the ATP generation in the CDs-fed group was 39.28 % (64.68 nmol/L) higher than that in the control group (46.44 nmol/L) (Fig. 5 a). Theoretically, the ATP production in the CDs-fed group decreased due to the absence of the complex (IV) process in the respiratory chain as a result of more oxygen being reduced extracellularly. The higher ATP production in the CDs-fed group implied that the electron transfer in Complex I, II, and III was enhanced to maintain the proton outflow or the proton motive force at a high level for the ATP generation.

In microbial respiration, protons motion from the inner cell to the periplasm was coupled with the electron transfer (PCET) along the respiration chain [37]. As the proton concentration increases in the periplasm, a trans-membrane potential (proton motive force) was generated. Then the trans-membrane potential was utilized by ATP synthase to convert ADP to ATP. Therefore, the PCET of respiratory enzymes was essential for ATP generation in respiration chains [39]. A strong PCET process in respiration chain could result in a higher bacterial ATP generation.

To confirm the enhanced proton outflow with electron transfer, a 2H/1H kinetic isotope effect (KIE) test was conducted to further investigate the effects of CDs on extracellular electron transfer. As shown in Fig. 5b, the initial current of the CDs-fed group was higher (2.039 × 10−9 A) than that of the control group before the addition of deuterated water (2H2O), indicating a higher conductivity of the bacteria after feeding the CDs. Noteworthily, the initial current of control (Fig. 5b) was likely a result of instinctive extracellular electron transfer with its secretion as the electron shuttle. After the addition of deuterated water (2H2O), the current of the CDs-fed group showed a continuous decrease and eventually maintained at a lower level than that before adding 2H2O, while the current of the control remained almost unchanged as the initial. The ratio of current values (IH / ID) before and after the addition of D2O in the CDs-fed group was 1.98; while the ratio of current values before and after the addition of D2O (IH / ID) was 1.04 in the control group with essentially unchanged current after the addition of D2O, indicating that heavy water addition had a more pronounced effect on the extracellular respiratory current in the CDs-fed group. With the addition of 2H2O, the higher mass of 2H replaced 1H (proton) of 1H2O to inevitably slow down the proton motion and electron transfer resulting in the current decrease. It indicated that the enhanced proton-coupling electron transfer after the CDs-fed, which led to the increased current generation and extracellular electron transfer, as well as the increase of ATP generation even detouring Complex IV due to the addition of CDs. Proton-coupling electron transfer, a critical process for the ATP generation of life, was enhanced with feeding CDs, which was a result of the more effective electron transfer for maintaining the ATP generation in the shortened respiration chain and for producing the •OH.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) tests showed that the internal resistance of the microbial biofilm decreased by 14.73 % (from 88.37 Ω to 75.35 Ω) with the CDs addition (Fig. 6 a), showing that the microbial biofilm was more conductive, which facilitated electron transfer to extracellular acceptors such as the electrode.

The electrical activity of individual bacteria was further evaluated by Conductive Atomic Force Microscopy (CAFM). The results showed that no electrical signal was detected in the bacteria cells in the control group, while an evident electrical signal (bright area in Fig. 6c) was found in the bacterial cells (at the cell wall) in the CDs-fed group. The electrical signals indicated the current generation during the scanning of bacterial aggregates by probes with the applied voltages. This implied that the addition of CDs did improve the electrical conductivity of the microbial aggregates, which benefited the more electron out-flow from bacteria cells to the extracellular electron acceptors for the microbial advanced oxidation.

The CV curve of CDs-fed B. subtilis and CDs-free B. subtilis were measured at N2 or O2 via coating them on electrodes, respectively. The integral area enclosed of I-V on the CV curve reflects the capacitance. Under the N2 aeration (Fig. 7 a), the capacitance of CDs-fed group was 31.1 % (2.74 μF) higher than the control group (2.09 μF), which provided more sites to transfer electrons to the extracellular acceptor. CDs might act as the nano conductor embedded in the cell, potentially mediating functional groups that were initially unable to participate in the electron transfer due to their distance from electron transfer sites, to enabling them to engage in electron transfer.

Under the N2 atmosphere, the current of the CDs-fed group was higher than that of the control group because the CDs enhanced the extracellular electron transfer of B. subtilis, resulting in an increase of electrons outflow from the intracellular to the extracellular. The currents of the two groups under the aerobic condition were significantly lower than those under the anaerobic condition. This is because oxygen competed with the electrodes for electrons to decrease the electric current generation. Noteworthily, after switching N2 to O2 aeration, the current decrement of the CD-fed group was greater than that of the CD-free group, which might be ascribed that oxygen has a stronger electron capturing capacity than the electrode, and the addition of CDs allowed more electrons to be captured by O2 (supplied with a voltage of 0 ∼ 0.2 V vs EO2/H2O2 = 0.695 V).

Accordingly, the electron-accepting capacity (EAC) test showed that the electronic receptivity of the CDs-fed group fed with CDs increased by 36.31 % compared to the control group (Fig. 7b). Noteworthily, no signal emerged in the EAC of CDs ((Fig. S3), indicating that the effect of CDs themselves on EAC was negligible. The significant increase in the biofilm capacitance in the experimental group might be ascribed to the CDs, as an electron bridge, enabling active functional groups away from electron transfer sites to be connected to electron transfer chains. Consistently, it has been reported that CDs can bind to protein-like substances and induce structural changes in their properties through hydrogen bonding interactions.

The absorption peaks at 1550 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1, representing C-N and C = O in the amide group, respectively, showed significant changes in the microbial aggregates of the CDs-fed group under the various test potentials (-0.80 V ∼ 0.20 V), while almost no changes were found in those of the CDs-free group. It indicated that the C-N and C = O bond of the amide group on the bacterial surfaces with the CDs addition were more inclined to be polarized.

The polarity is positively related to the relative permittivity (i.e., the relative capacitance) of the substance. Considering that the electric potential is a prevalent force to drive electron transfer in life activities, the feeding of CDs might allow the capacitance of B. subtilis to increase by inducing the polarization of the amide group during electron transfer. At the same time, the polarization of C = O also increased the electronegativity of the oxygen atoms to attract more protons from nearby chemical bonds such as N-H, which likely broke the chemical bonds to allow electron transfer with the coupling of protons (i.e, proton-coupled electron transfer), thus improving the electrical conductivity of aggregates (Fig. 8 ).

Natural and artificial processes produce numerous conductive nanomaterials similar to CDs, such as carbon fibers and grapheme [40–42]. The impact of these materials on pollutant transformation is manifold, including adsorption, co-precipitation, photo/biological chemical conversion [43–45], etc. However, the study of these materials on intervening microbial extracellular respiration and generation of reactive free radicals has not been investigated yet. The present study showed that the conductive nanomaterial might play a crucial role in the decomposition of pollutants in redox shock conditions such as coastal zones, wetlands and soils.

Microbial advanced oxidation, based on microbial extracellular respiration, is a critical process for pollutant removal in nature [1–3]. However, most microorganisms, like B. subtilis, lack electron transfer components such as MtrCAB in the cell wall and usually rely on electron shuttles for extracellular respiration [21]. However, the thicker peptidoglycan in the cell wall hinders the diffusion of electron shuttles and impedes cross-wall electron transfer [22], which restocts microbial extracellular respiration as well as microbial advanced oxidation [44].

In this study, we found that CDs could be embedded in the cell wall of bacterial cells to enhance the cross-wall electron transport of B. subtilis and promote the •OH generation. Notably, the embedded CDs induced oxygen reduction to occur extracellularly, thereby decreasing the •OH generation intracellularly. Feeding CDs provided a possible strategy to avoid radical damages caused by respiration to cell.

In summary, feeding carbon quantum dots (CDs) accelerated microbial extracellular respiration to strengthen the microbial advanced oxidation of aniline. The embedded CDs not only served as the bridge for cross-wall electron transfer to make the radical generation extracellularly and reduce the damage to bacterial cells. Notably, CDs seemed to be doped with the cell wall to affect the structure of amide groups in the cell wall and thereby inproving the cross-wall electron transfer properties. In this way. The proton-coupled electron transfer during respiration was finnally enhanced, which enabled vigorous ATP generation of B. subtilis. Feeding CDs seems to be a reliable way to strengthen the wake extracellular respiration of common microorganisms in nature to promote bioremediation via microbial advanced oxidation.

Ziyang Zhang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Chang Yang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xingyuan Song: Data curation. Qilin Yu: Writing – review & editing. Zhiqiang Zhao: Formal analysis. Huimin Zhao: Formal analysis. Yaobin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program Projects of China (2023YFD1701401).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.149102.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
